skill.name: smtp-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized SMTP security assessments, including service discovery, banner review, relay validation, authentication checks, and remediation planning.
skill.summary: Assess SMTP mail server exposure and hardening gaps during authorized security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true positives for network testing and external command usage. The skill contains authorized-use language, but it also provides operational SMTP enumeration, open relay testing, spoofing, Metasploit, and password attack workflows, so it should not be published without substantial safety revision.

high_findings.title: Operational User Enumeration and Credential Attack Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill gives runnable workflows for SMTP user enumeration and authentication attacks using smtp-user-enum, Nmap scripts, Hydra, Medusa, and Metasploit. These are legitimate in authorized tests, but they can directly support account discovery and credential compromise against mail servers.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly labels a user enumeration scenario as phishing preparation and provides concrete tool commands. The same section includes password attack tooling with user and password wordlists.

high_findings.title: Open Relay Exploitation and Email Spoofing Examples
high_findings.description: The skill includes manual SMTP message sequences and tool commands for testing relay abuse, injected headers, blind carbon-copy insertion, and spoofed executive email content. This can be used for spam relay validation or social engineering if not constrained to authorized environments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples include external recipients, attacker sender names, injected headers, and spoofed business email content. The surrounding authorization warnings reduce malicious intent certainty but do not remove operational abuse risk.

high_findings.title: Metasploit SMTP Modules and Network Scanner Workflows
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run Metasploit SMTP auxiliary modules and Nmap SMTP scripts for enumeration, relay testing, and login checks. These are high-risk offensive tools in a marketplace skill, even when the intended use is assessment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple Metasploit modules are named with RHOSTS and wordlist configuration. This confirms the static Metasploit and network scanning findings as true positives.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Execution Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill is not executable code, but it repeatedly instructs the user to run shell commands, install offensive tools with sudo, and connect to network services. This raises operational risk because an AI assistant may execute commands against user-supplied targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static external command detections are true positives for command examples, not hidden command execution in code. The risk remains meaningful because the commands perform network reconnaissance and testing.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Reconnaissance and Banner Collection
medium_findings.description: The service discovery and banner grabbing sections collect software versions, hostnames, supported SMTP extensions, MX records, and cipher information. This is normal for authorized audits, but the same data can help target unapproved systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions are semantically aligned with reconnaissance and configuration review. The skill includes authorization language, so this is medium risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Private Example IP Addresses Are Not Exfiltration Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP address detections refer to private lab-style addresses in an example output. No evidence found that these addresses are used for command and control, exfiltration, or a real external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are 192.168.1.100 examples inside a documented user enumeration scenario. They are still part of offensive guidance, but the hardcoded IP finding itself is a false positive for external infrastructure.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are Contextual or False Positive
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings appear tied to phrases about weak authentication and SMTP response code documentation, not to code that implements deprecated cryptography. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or insecure cipher selection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is descriptive audit terminology rather than application code. TLS testing is covered elsewhere, but the reported weak cryptographic algorithm pattern is not confirmed as an implementation flaw.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Brute Force Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: Hydra, Medusa, and Metasploit login modules are presented for SMTP authentication testing with user and password lists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly configure credential guessing against SMTP services. This is a clear dangerous dual-use pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: SMTP User Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches VRFY, EXPN, RCPT, smtp-user-enum, Nmap, and Metasploit methods to identify valid email accounts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content explicitly describes valid user discovery and shows resulting usernames. This confirms the enumeration risk with high confidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Open Relay and Spoofed Email Procedures
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides message flows and scanner commands that test external delivery, header injection, and spoofed sender behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The procedures are concrete and operational. Authorization warnings are present, but the abuse pathway is still clearly demonstrated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Scanner Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly uses Nmap SMTP scripts for service discovery, user enumeration, open relay checks, and cipher enumeration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scanner commands are explicit and target network services. They are normal in penetration testing but risky in public marketplace content.

content.value_statement: Mail servers often expose relay, identity, and authentication weaknesses. This skill organizes authorized SMTP assessment steps and remediation guidance for controlled security reviews.
actual_capabilities: Explains SMTP architecture, common ports, and mail flow concepts.
Guides service discovery, banner review, and SMTP capability checks.
Describes methods for assessing user enumeration exposure.
Covers open relay validation and documentation for authorized tests.
Reviews STARTTLS, SMTPS, SPF, DKIM, and DMARC assessment steps.
limitations: It is dual-use and requires explicit written authorization before any testing.
It does not verify scope, ownership, or legal permission by itself.
It may trigger monitoring, rate limits, lockouts, or abuse controls.
Some examples are too operational for unrestricted marketplace publication.

use_cases.title: Authorized Mail Server Audit
use_cases.description: Assess an owned SMTP service for exposed commands, relay behavior, authentication policy, and TLS configuration.

use_cases.title: Email Infrastructure Hardening Review
use_cases.description: Map SMTP findings to practical fixes such as disabling VRFY, enforcing authentication, and improving SPF, DKIM, and DMARC.

use_cases.title: Compliance Evidence Preparation
use_cases.description: Organize SMTP security observations into a clear assessment report with findings, impact, and remediation actions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Authorized SMTP Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized SMTP security review for my organization. Include scope checks, safe test categories, and reporting sections.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret SMTP Exposure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these SMTP assessment observations from an authorized test and explain the security impact, likely causes, and safe remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Mail Server Fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized hardening plan for an SMTP server with user enumeration exposure, weak relay controls, and incomplete email authentication records.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Executive Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn authorized SMTP assessment notes into an executive summary, technical findings, risk ratings, and remediation owners without adding exploit steps.

output_examples.input: An administrator asks for help reviewing STARTTLS and email authentication posture.
output_examples.output: A structured review plan covering supported ports, TLS behavior, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, observed gaps, risk impact, and remediation priorities.

output_examples.input: A security engineer provides authorized SMTP findings showing VRFY exposure and relay rejection behavior.
output_examples.output: A concise assessment that explains user enumeration risk, confirms relay controls, and recommends disabling exposed commands and improving monitoring.

output_examples.input: A risk team needs a non-technical summary of mail server weaknesses.
output_examples.output: A business-focused summary describing account discovery risk, spoofing controls, transport security, and practical ownership for remediation.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, targets, and testing windows before any SMTP interaction.
Prefer passive review and configuration evidence before active probing or authentication testing.
Report findings with impact, evidence, remediation steps, and validation criteria.
anti_patterns: Do not test third-party mail servers without explicit permission.
Do not send real spam, phishing content, or unwanted messages during relay checks.
Do not perform password attacks unless scope, accounts, lockout rules, and rate limits are approved.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Not in its current form. It contains operational enumeration, relay, spoofing, and credential attack procedures.

faq.question: What authorization is required?
faq.answer: Use it only for systems you own or where written permission explicitly covers SMTP testing activities.

faq.question: Can it help with defensive hardening?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes remediation themes for relay control, user enumeration prevention, TLS, authentication records, and monitoring.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide, but it contains many shell commands and tool workflows.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level high?
faq.answer: The content can directly support account discovery, credential attacks, spoofing tests, and relay abuse if misused.

faq.question: What would make it safer?
faq.answer: Remove exploit-style command sequences, focus on defensive configuration review, and require authorization and rate-limit safeguards.
